title: Reload friction
principles: Reload friction must be evaluated from the complete firing cycle rather than from reload time alone.
Compare reload time with the approximate active firing window before the magazine is consumed.
Reloading becomes more disruptive when inactive reload time occupies a large share of repeated attack cycles.
High fire rate may improve immediate pressure while consuming the magazine sooner and increasing reload frequency.
A large magazine extends active firing time and reduces reload frequency, but it does not shorten each interruption.
Reload speed reduces inactive time directly, while magazine size mainly delays when that inactive time occurs.
A small magazine can create strong reload friction even when listed reload time is only moderate.
A large magazine may tolerate a longer reload when active firing time is substantially longer than the interruption.
Do not classify reload time as universally good or bad using a fixed duration threshold.
Do not assume fire rate is a pure sustained-output improvement when it also increases magazine consumption and reload frequency.
Conventional magazine, shell-by-shell, staged, battery, and automatic-recharge systems must be evaluated separately.
Interruptible shell-by-shell reloads may reduce practical friction because partial recovery is possible.
Battery weapons require separation between recharge delay and recharge duration when those values are supplied.
Reload improvements primarily improve continuity and usability, but they can materially affect combat performance when downtime occupies a substantial portion of the cycle.

low: The active firing window is much longer than the reload interruption.
Reload downtime represents a small share of the cycle.
moderate: Reloading is noticeable but does not dominate the cycle.
Additional fire rate may increase reload frequency noticeably.
high: Reload downtime occupies a large share of the cycle.
Repeated reload interruptions are likely to break sustained pressure.
special_reload: The reload system cannot be evaluated reliably with conventional full-magazine logic alone.
Battery or shell-by-shell behavior requires its specific supplied fields.
undetermined: The supplied data is insufficient to compare active firing time with reload downtime.

exceptions: Do not apply conventional magazine-duration logic directly to battery weapons.
Do not treat shell-by-shell total duration as one unavoidable interruption when it can be interrupted.
Do not infer practical fire rate when charge, burst delay, spool-up, or manual input limits it.
Do not assume multishot increases ammunition consumption unless explicitly supplied.
